title: Chapter XVI.

source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Upon this, Postumianus exclaims, “Let that well-known man in our immediate neighborhood, listen to that example, who, when he is wise, takes no notice either of things present or future, but if he has been offended, falls into utter fury, having no control over himself.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: He then rages against the clerics, and makes bitter attacks upon the laity, while he stirs up the whole world for his own revenge.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: He will continue in this state of contention for three years without intermission, and refuse to be mollified either by time or reason.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: The condition of the man is to be lamented and pitied, even if this were the only incurable evil by which he is afflicted.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: But you ought, my Gallic friend, to have frequently recalled to his mind such examples of patience and tranquillity, that he might know both how to be angry and how to forgive.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: And if he happens to hear of this speech of mine which has been briefly interpolated into our discourse, and. directed against himself, let him know that I spoke, not more with the lips of an enemy than the mind of a friend; because I should wish, if the thing were possible, that he should be spoken of rather as being like the bishop Martin, than the tyrant Phalaris.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: But let us pass away from him, since the mention of him is far from pleasant, and let us return, O Gaul, to our friend Martin.”
